request.question: In which airport is the head office of the airline which joined with the government of Sierra Leone to form Leone Airways?

title: Tower Hill (Sierra Leone)
Patrick Omolade Hamilton
Oku people (Sierra Leone)
Government of Sierra Leone
Leone Airways
Special Court for Sierra Leone
Mohamed Sillah
Parliament of Sierra Leone
Arik Air
Sierra Leone Anti-corruption Commission
sentences: Tower Hill is an affluent government and residential neighborhood in central Freetown, Sierra Leone.
Tower Hill is located within the central business district in downtown Freetown.
Tower Hill is the seat of the government of Sierra Leone, as it is home to the State House (the office and residence of the Sierra Leone president).

Tower Hill is also home to the Sierra Leone House of Parliament, the Sierra Leone Ministry of Defence, the Bank of Sierra Leone , The Sierra Leone National Electoral Commission (NEC) , and many other government buildings.
Patrick Omolade Hamilton is a Sierra Leone judge and an associate justice in the Supreme Court of Sierra Leone.
He had previously served as a judge in the Sierra Leone High Court and the Sierra Leone Court of Appeal.
He was appointed as a Supreme Court justice by Sierra Leone's president Ernest Bai Koroma and took office on January 22, 2009 after being confirmed by the Sierra Leone Parliament.
The Oku people, also commonly known as Oku Mohammedans or "Aku Mohammedans"in Sierra Leone and as the "Aku Marabou" or "Oku Marabou" in the Gambia, are an ethnic group in Sierra Leone and the Gambia.

The Oku people are the descendants of liberated Africans of Yoruba descent from Southwest Nigeria who were liberated or came to Sierra Leone as settlers in the mid 19th century and formed a distinctive ethnic group The Oku are virtually all Muslims and are known for their conservative muslim population.
The British colonial government provided official recognition to the Oku Mohammedan community as a distinctive community in Sierra Leone.
Although the Sierra Leone government officially considered the Oku people as members of the Creole ethnic group, many Sierra Leoneans consider the Oku people as a distinctive ethnic group.
The government of Sierra Leone is the governing authority of the Republic of Sierra Leone, as established by the Sierra Leone Constitution.
The Sierra Leone government is divided into three branches: the executive, legislative and the judicial.
The seat of government of Sierra Leone is in the capital Freetown.
Leone Airways was a planned national carrier for Sierra Leone.
It was established as a joint venture between the government of Sierra Leone and Arik Airlines of Nigeria.

The Special Court for Sierra Leone, or the "Special Court" (SCSL), also called the Sierra Leone Tribunal, was a judicial body set up by the government of Sierra Leone and the United Nations to "prosecute persons who bear the greatest responsibility for serious violations of international humanitarian law and Sierra Leonean law" committed in Sierra Leone after 30 November 1996 and during the Sierra Leone Civil War.
The court's working language was English.
The court listed offices in Freetown, The Hague, and New York City.
Mohamed Yahya Sillah was born December 12, 1949 in Massam Kpaka, Pujehun District, Republic of Sierra Leone.
His father, Alhaji Yahya Sillah, now 100 years old, served as Regent Chief (Acting Paramount Chief) for several years in Dama Chiefdom, Kenema District in the early seventies.
Alhaji Yahya Sillah was appointed to that high position by Sierra Leone President Siaka Stevens.
His mother, Haja Mamie Zoe Mansaray, was a home maker.
A journalist and human rights activist, Mohamed Yahya Sillah was a vocal torch-bearer in the transition efforts from military to civilian rule in Sierra Leone, 1996.

As Leader and National Chairman of National Alliance Democratic Party (NADP), he competed the 1996 Presidential elections in Sierra Leone, becoming one of only thirteen political leaders that qualified to contest the Presidential and Parliamentary elections in the country at the age of 46.
His adoring personality lured many Sierra Leoneans and media practitioners to view him as one of the most eloquent and charismatic politicians in Sierra Leone.
Nevertheless, he received only 0.5% of the votes (3,723 total votes), good for 13th place.
Although he protested the election results on BBC's Focus on Africa, he later suspended his protest "for the sake of peace."
Mohamed Yahya Sillah continues to enjoy massive support from the youth and women supporters in Sierra Leone.
In 2007, he withdrew his bid for national leadership in the Presidential and Parliamentary elections, citing inadequate funding and ineffective campaign management machinery.
He later put his weight behind the Sierra Leone Peoples Party (SLPP) presidential candidate, Vice President Solomon Berewa.
When Berewa lost the elections, Mohamed Yahya Sillah gracefully congratulated the winning candidate, Ernest Bai Koroma, of the All People's Congress (APC) party.
Mohamed Yahya Sillah vows to respect and help protect the dignity of any Sierra Leonean that is legitimately and democratically elected to office by the people of Sierra Leone.
Parliament of Sierra Leone is the legislative branch of the government of Sierra Leone.
It is principally responsible for making laws.
The Sierra Leone parliament consists of 124 members, of which 112 members are directly elected from across Sierra Leone's 14 Districts.
The Parliament is led by the Speaker.
The current speaker of Parliament is Sheku Badara Bashiru Dumbuya (APC).
The current elected members of parliament are entirely composed of members of the All People's Congress and the Sierra Leone People's Party, the two largest political parties in Sierra Leone.
Arik Air is a Nigerian airline operating mainly from two hubs at Murtala Muhammed International Airport near Lagos and Nnamdi Azikiwe International Airport in Abuja.
Arik Air's head office is the "Arik Air Aviation Center" on the grounds of Murtala Muhammed International Airport in Ikeja.
Arik Air serves a network of regional and mid-haul destinations within Africa.

The Sierra Leone Anti-corruption Commission (ACC-SL) commonly known as ACC is an independent agency of the Sierra Leone Sierra Leone Government, that investigates and prosecutes corruption cases in Sierra Leone.
The ACC is supervised by the Sierra Leone Ministry of Justice.
The current Head of the ACC is Ady Macauley, who has been in office since March 14, 2016.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: In which airport is the head office of the airline which joined with the government of Sierra Leone to form Leone Airways?
hop1_docs: Leone Airways | Leone Airways was a planned national carrier for Sierra Leone.  It was established as a joint venture between the government of Sierra Leone and Arik Airlines of Nigeria.

West African Airways Corporation | West African Airways Corporation, or WAAC for short, was an airline that operated from 1946 to 1958, jointly owned by the governments of Britain's four west African colonies, namely The Gambia, the Gold Coast (now Ghana), Nigeria, and Sierra Leone.  The carrier was headquartered at the "Airways House" in Ikeja, Nigeria, and operated from its hub in Lagos Airport.  It was dissolved on none }} , after all the shareholder countries but Nigeria set up their own national airlines following their independence.  As the sole remaining major stockholder of the airline, the government of Nigeria continued to operate it as WAAC Nigeria, which was eventually renamed Nigeria Airways and became the flag carrier of the country.

Peoples Party (Sierra Leone) | The Peoples Party of Sierra Leone was a political party formed by Lamina Sankoh in 1948.  The party sought to unite the British Crown Colony of Freetown with the protectorate of Sierra Leone, which were governed separately until independence in 1961.  The party merged with 2 other organizations (The Protectorate Education Progressive Union and The Sierra Leone Organisation Society) in 1951 to form the Sierra Leone Peoples Party (SLPP), which is today one of the two major parties in the government of the country.

President of Sierra Leone | The President of the Republic of Sierra Leone is the head of state and the head of government of Sierra Leone, as well as the commander-in-chief of the Armed Forces.  The President appoints and heads a Cabinet of ministers (including the Vice President), which must be approved by the Parliament.

Sierra National Airlines | Sierra National Airlines was the national airline of Sierra Leone.  It was based at the Lungi International Airport, in Lungi, Sierra Leone.  It operated scheduled domestic, regional and international services.
Fly 6ix | Fly 6ix is a former passenger airline with its head office in Freetown, Sierra Leone, operating out of Lungi International Airport.

Sierra Leone Anti-corruption Commission | The Sierra Leone Anti-corruption Commission (ACC-SL) commonly known as ACC is an independent agency of the Sierra Leone Sierra Leone Government, that investigates and prosecutes corruption cases in Sierra Leone.  The ACC is supervised by the Sierra Leone Ministry of Justice.  The current Head of the ACC is Ady Macauley, who has been in office since March 14, 2016.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Leone Airways: "Leone Airways was a planned national carrier for Sierra Leone." (passage [1])
  - Partner airline: "Arik Airlines of Nigeria" (quoted token: "Arik Airlines of Nigeria") (passage [1])
- Ambiguities:
  - The passage names the partner as "Arik Airlines" (passage [1]). Common variants/pages may be titled "Arik Air" or "Arik Airlines (Nigeria)"; no Arik page/variant is present in the supplied passages.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (exactly as in passages): Arik Airlines
  - Missing fact needed: the name of the airport in which Arik Airlines' head office is located (e.g., specific airport name hosting its head office)
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open the airline's page(s): "Arik Air" and "Arik Airlines" (check for redirects). Inspect the lede and the infobox for "Headquarters"/"Head office" or location lines.
     - Search keywords: "Arik Air head office airport", "Arik Airlines headquarters airport".
  2) If the main article lacks detail, open sections titled "Corporate affairs", "Contact", or "Headquarters"; also check the page's infobox for an airport name.
  3) If ambiguous, fetch "Arik (disambiguation)" or "Arik Air (Nigeria)" and any official company/contact pages linked from the article.
  4) If necessary, open the specific airport pages returned (e.g., candidate airports in Lagos/Ikeja) to confirm the head-office is located at that airport.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
hop2_docs: Arik Air | Arik Air is a Nigerian airline operating mainly from two hubs at Murtala Muhammed International Airport near Lagos and Nnamdi Azikiwe International Airport in Abuja.  Arik Air's head office is the "Arik Air Aviation Center" on the grounds of Murtala Muhammed International Airport in Ikeja.  Arik Air serves a network of regional and mid-haul destinations within Africa.

Air Alps | Air Alps (Air Alps Aviation, Alpenländisches Flugunternehmen Ges.m.b.H.)  was an airline with its head office at Innsbruck Airport in Innsbruck, Austria.  It was an independent regional airline serving 10 cities in Italy from Milan and Bolzano, international services from Milan, as well as charter services.  Its main base was Innsbruck Airport, with hub at Malpensa Airport, Milan.  Formerly its head office was elsewhere in Innsbruck.

Dushanbe International Airport | Dushanbe International Airport (IATA: DYU, ICAO: UTDD) is an airport in Dushanbe, the capital of Tajikistan.  Dushanbe Airport is a main hub for Somon Air and Tajik Air.  Tajik Air has its head office on the property.

Sadiq Abubakar III International Airport | Sadiq Abubakar III International Airport or Sultan Saddik Abubakar Airport (IATA: SKO, ICAO: DNSO) is an airport serving Sokoto, the capital of the Sokoto State in Nigeria.  Arik Air does daily operation from Abuja and Med-View Airlines does flights to Jeddah during Hajj using a 747.
Arik Niger | Arik Niger was the Nigerien subsidiary of Arik Air based in Niamey, Niger.  The airline suspended operations in February 2010.
Arik Air destinations | Arik Air flies to 13 domestic destinations and 10 international destinations as of March 2017.

Barrier Air | Barrier Air is a New Zealand airline that was established in 1983 by Jim Bergman as Great Barrier Airlines.  The head office is located at the Domestic Terminal at Auckland Airport in Mangere, with additional offices in the terminal buildings at Great Barrier Aerodrome, Kaitaia Airport and North Shore Aerodrome.
response.summary_2: The head office is located at Murtala Muhammed International Airport. Arik Air's head office is the "Arik Air Aviation Center" on the grounds of Murtala Muhammed International Airport in Ikeja.
